name: Moving Average Convergence Divergence
description_simple: MACD measures the distance between two moving averages of price, revealing whether short-term momentum is accelerating or decelerating relative to the longer-term trend.
description_detailed:
MACD, developed by Gerald Appel in the late 1970s, captures momentum by taking the difference between a fast and a slow Exponential Moving Average. When the fast EMA (12-period) is above the slow EMA (26-period), MACD is positive, indicating that short-term momentum is bullish. A 9-period EMA of the MACD line itself, called the Signal line, then smooths the MACD to produce a trigger. The Histogram, the visual bar chart below the zero line, shows the distance between MACD and its Signal line — widening bars indicate accelerating momentum, and narrowing bars signal deceleration.

MACDs three components carry distinct analytical value. The MACD line crossing above zero confirms that the shorter-term trend has overtaken the longer-term trend — a macro momentum shift. The MACD line crossing above the Signal line is a faster, more sensitive signal used for shorter-term entries. The Histogram is the most sensitive component: because it measures the rate of change of MACD itself (momentum of momentum), Histogram divergence from price — where price reaches a new high but the Histogram is lower than its previous peak — is considered one of the earliest warning signs of trend exhaustion available to technical traders.

The standard 12/26/9 parameters were selected by Appel for weekly data. On daily charts, these parameters have become the de facto standard, but traders running intraday strategies often compress the parameters (e.g., 5/13/4 or 3/10/16) to reduce lag. The relative nature of the indicator — it measures spread between two averages rather than price level — means MACD can be used across assets with vastly different price levels without rescaling.
formula:
MACD Line = EMA(Close, fast) - EMA(Close, slow)
Signal Line = EMA(MACD Line, signal)
Histogram = MACD Line - Signal Line

Default: fast=12, slow=26, signal=9

fast.description: Period for the fast EMA. Controls how quickly MACD responds to recent price changes. Shorter values increase sensitivity.

slow.description: Period for the slow EMA. Represents the longer-term baseline trend. Must be greater than the fast period.

signal.description: Period for the EMA smoothing of the MACD line to create the Signal line. Shorter values produce faster crossover signals.

how_traders_use_it: Signal line crossover: MACD line crossing above the Signal line is a buy signal; crossing below is a sell signal. Most commonly used entry/exit trigger for MACD-based systems.
Zero line crossover: MACD crossing from negative to positive (zero line cross up) confirms the short-term trend has shifted bullish; used as a macro trend filter.
Histogram divergence: price making a new high while the Histogram prints a lower peak signals bullish momentum is weakening — a leading warning to exit longs or prepare for reversal.
Histogram expansion/contraction: increasing Histogram bars in the direction of the trend confirm trend acceleration; shrinking bars warn of deceleration before a signal line crossover occurs.
Trend identification: sustained positive MACD with the Histogram consistently above zero is a clean confirmation that an uptrend is in force and long bias is warranted.
pros: Combines trend direction (zero line position) and momentum timing (signal line crossovers) in a single indicator, reducing the need for multiple separate tools.
The Histogram provides a visual, leading read on momentum acceleration and deceleration, often warning of reversals before the price chart does.
Scale-independent: because MACD is a difference of averages, it works across assets with very different price magnitudes without normalization.
Three distinct components (MACD, Signal, Histogram) give traders flexibility to use the most appropriate signal for their strategy timeframe and risk tolerance.
cons: A lagging indicator built from two lagging EMAs: by the time a MACD crossover occurs, a significant portion of the move has already happened.
Frequent false signals in sideways, choppy markets where the fast and slow EMAs repeatedly converge and diverge without directional follow-through.
The 12/26/9 defaults were designed for weekly charts; applying them to intraday charts without adjustment often produces signals that are too slow to be actionable.
MACD does not have fixed bounds like RSI, making overbought/oversold readings relative rather than absolute — comparing extreme MACD values across different market regimes is not straightforward.
common_mistakes: Taking every Signal line crossover as a trade signal without a trend filter: in ranging markets, crossovers fire constantly and most will lose money.
Using MACD Histogram divergence without price structure context: a lower Histogram peak only warns of weakening momentum, not an imminent reversal — confirmation from price action is required.

Applying the standard 12/26/9 parameters to all timeframes without adjustment: a 5-minute chart MACD with these settings uses EMAs spanning over 2 hours of data, which is too slow for most intraday strategies.

Confusing the Histogram crossing zero with the MACD line crossing zero: the Histogram zero cross simply means MACD and Signal are equal, which is a much less significant event than the MACD line crossing the zero line itself.
when_it_fails: Volatile, trendless markets: rapid oscillations around a flat average cause the fast and slow EMAs to repeatedly cross, generating MACD signals in both directions with no sustained follow-through.
During gap moves: overnight or pre-market gaps cause the MACD to jump discontinuously, producing a false signal at the open before the indicator can re-stabilize.
During very slow, grinding trends: when price trends at a near-constant rate, the fast and slow EMAs stay nearly parallel and MACD hovers near a flat value, producing no actionable crossover signals.
advanced_quant_note: MACD is mathematically equivalent to the difference of two IIR low-pass filters applied to the price series, which means it acts as a band-pass filter isolating cyclical components in the frequency range between the two EMA cutoff frequencies. For standard 12/26 parameters, this band corresponds roughly to cycles with periods between 12 and 26 bars. The Signal line applies a second IIR pass to further smooth the output, making the Histogram a second-difference (acceleration) measure of price. From a quantitative perspective, MACD divergence is an early warning because price momentum tends to lead price levels: the first derivative of price (momentum) peaks and troughs systematically before the price level itself in mean-reverting assets, a property directly exploitable via Histogram peak analysis.
